title: Chapter 22: The Sabotage Plan
content: Liu Haoran's new location was a defunct printing press in the industrial district—not a tea house, but a warehouse of rusted machinery and dust that smelled of old ink and older secrets. He'd set up a command station in the back office: multiple screens, array detection equipment, and a coffee maker that looked like it had survived three wars.
"Qian Delun's equipment," Liu said, gesturing at the array detectors. "Hong liberated it from his apartment before the entity could destroy it. Some of it still works."

Ren looked at the screens. They were displaying dimensional frequency maps of Crystal City—heat signatures overlaid on street grids, showing the seven-dimensional resonance of the Liang estate's array network. The visualization was crude compared to the Salvation System's internal rendering, but it was independent verification. A second opinion.
"The demonstration." Ren got straight to business. "Xue is the intended channeler. I need to stop it."

Liu nodded. He was thinner than Ren had imagined—not gaunt, but pared down, as if the process of dying and returning had burned away everything nonessential. His eyes were sharp, though. And when he spoke, it was with the precision of someone who'd spent the afterlife studying the enemy.

"The demonstration uses a three-node configuration: Nodes 1, 2, and 5, arranged in a triangular resonance pattern. The Patriarch will channel the primary frequency. A second channeler—Xue, if Feng can't hold the line—provides the bloodline amplification. The triangle creates a controlled aperture, approximately two meters in diameter, stable for no more than thirty seconds."
"Long enough for Zhao Kang to see through it."

"Long enough for Zhao Kang to see *something* looking back." Liu pulled up a schematic on the central screen. "The aperture is small, but it's real. A genuine dimensional breach. The entity on the other side will cooperate—show Kang something impressive enough to restore his investment confidence. Then the aperture closes, Kang writes a check, and the Patriarch gets his funding back."
"And Xue?"

"Dead. Channeling the amplification frequency is like standing in front of a fire hose. The bloodline carries the current, but the current burns. Without War God-level power to buffer the energy—your 1.3% isn't enough—the channeler's nervous system cooks from the inside out."

Ren absorbed this. The tactical picture was clear: he couldn't stop the demonstration by force, couldn't substitute himself for Xue (he wasn't a bloodline channeler), and couldn't warn Zhao Kang (the elder had gone dark after the data leak).
But he could *sabotage the array*.
"The three-node configuration," he said. "It requires precise harmonic alignment. If one of the nodes is off-frequency—even slightly—the resonance triangle won't form."

"Exactly." Liu touched the screen, highlighting the Bronze Bell—Node 5. "The Bell is currently in calibration. Your corruption has already drifted it 0.3% toward containment mode. If you can increase that drift to 2% before the demonstration, the triangle won't align. The aperture will fail. And a failed demonstration in front of Zhao Kang destroys the Patriarch's credibility more thoroughly than any data leak."
"How much time do I have to increase the drift?"

"Forty-six days. Five more injections at the current rate should bring you to 2%. But—" Liu's expression tightened. "Each injection requires physical access to the Bell. With the Patriarch on high alert after the Zhao fallout, the restricted wing's security has tripled. Two guards at every checkpoint. Array sensors calibrated to detect any unauthorized bloodline signature."
"I have bloodline access. Xue's been inside before."
"Xue is the Patriarch's target now. If she enters the restricted wing again, he'll know exactly why."
"Which means we need someone he *doesn't* suspect."
Liu's eyes met his. "Feng."

"Feng. Spore-compromised, but in a dormant phase. Bloodline access. And recently reconciled with his father—or at least pretending to be. If Feng enters the restricted wing, the Patriarch might assume he's there for the supplements."
"The supplements that are poisoning him."
"Which he's still taking. Reduced dose, as I recommended. But the Patriarch doesn't know that Feng is consciously titrating. As far as Tianming knows, his son is a compliant vessel-in-training."
Liu processed this. Then, slowly, he nodded.
"Feng carries the injection. But he doesn't have the War God's frequency modulation ability. How does he deliver it?"

"The jade token." Ren pulled the Qian bloodline keepsake from his pocket. "It's been imprinted with the maintenance frequency. Anyone with bloodline sensitivity can activate the imprint—the token does the rest. Feng carries it into the junction chamber, places it against the Bell for thirty seconds, and leaves. The drift compounds."
"And if the entity inside Feng takes over during the operation?"

"That's the risk." Ren met Liu's gaze straight-on. "Feng at Stage 2 can resist the entity for minutes at a time—especially when the stakes are clear. He'll have a five-minute window of full lucidity after he takes the emergency suppressant I'll give him. After that, the entity resurfaces. He needs to be in and out within five minutes."
"And if he's not?"
"Then we lose a node, an ally, and possibly Xue. But we don't lose the war, because Node 4 stays locked."

It was cold calculus. The kind of calculation that generals made and soldiers died for. Ren felt the weight of it—the probability of Feng's survival factored against the certainty of Xue's death if the demonstration proceeded.
"The suppressant," Liu said. "What is it?"

"A frequency burst. The Salvation System can generate a one-second pulse at the containment resonance, strong enough to shock the entity into dormancy for approximately five minutes. The pulse will come through the jade token—it's already frequency-attuned." Ren turned the token in his fingers. "When Feng touches the Bell, the token delivers the maintenance injection AND the suppressant pulse simultaneously. Two operations, one contact."

"Clever." Liu studied him with an expression that was difficult to read—not respect, exactly, but something adjacent to it. "You were a general. A real one. Not just a warrior—a *strategist*. In my timeline, you held the Eastern Gate with seven people against a tide that should have washed over you in seconds. You held it for *eleven minutes*."
"Twelve, actually. The last three were just me."

"Eleven or twelve, it was the longest anyone had ever held a dimensional breach. And watching you—now, here, planning this operation—I can see how you did it. Not by being the strongest, but by being the one who could see the most moves ahead."

"I'm not seeing enough moves." Ren pocketed the token. "The demonstration is forty-six days away. The sabotage is one operation among dozens. And behind all of it, the entity is playing its own game—visiting my dreams, turning my allies, undermining my confidence." He paused. "It mentioned you, specifically. Said you'd seen something on the other side that changed you."
Liu was quiet for a long moment. The screens cast shifting light across his face—a man caught between two truths.

"I saw the multiverse," he said finally. "All of it. Every dimension, every reality, every version of every person who'd ever lived. And yes—some of it was beautiful. Vast beyond comprehension. *True* in ways that human truth can't touch." He met Ren's eyes. "I also saw what the entity does to worlds it consumes. Not evolution. Not transcendence. *Digestion*. The slow, methodical dissolution of everything that makes a world unique, reduced to raw dimensional energy that feeds the entity's expansion."
"And the truth it told you?"

"The entity doesn't lie. It just... omits. Shows you the beautiful parts. The seven weak points on Earth. The possibility of transcendence. The futility of resistance. All true, from a certain angle." Liu's voice hardened. "But the angle matters, Zhou. A truth that only shows you one side of the coin isn't a lie—it's a manipulation. And I came back knowing the difference."
"How can I be sure?"

"You can't. Just like I can't be sure you're really the War God and not the entity wearing his face. We're operating on trust, which is the weakest foundation in any war—but sometimes it's the only one available." Liu stood. "I've given you the array configuration, the demonstration timeline, and the sabotage method. What you do with it is your call. But if you're wasting time wondering whether I've been compromised, you're losing ground to a Patriarch who doesn't doubt anything."
*He's right. Paralysis by analysis is just another form of defeat.*
Ren stood. Extended his hand.
"Partners."
Liu clasped it. His grip was firm but cold—the temperature of someone whose circulation didn't work quite the way it used to.
"Partners. Until the breach is sealed or we're both dead."
"I've had worse terms."
On the way back to the estate, Ren sent a message to Feng:
*"Token incoming. Tomorrow evening, garden. Five-minute briefing. Tell no one."*
Feng's reply came in thirty seconds:
*"Understood."*
The pieces were moving. The board was set. In forty-six days, the Patriarch would demonstrate his portal to the man he'd betrayed, using his daughter as fuel for the fire.
Unless Ren Zhou, last War God of humanity, with 1.3% of his power and a jade token that belonged to a dead man, could break the resonance triangle before it formed.
The countdown pulsed:
`1068 days`
But the real countdown was forty-six days. And it was already ticking.
